brief.name: RE-1 — boutique off-market acquisitions team (LA)  // JOHNNY-EDITABLE name/handle
brief.what_they_do: Founder-led team (2-5) doing off-market / distressed acquisitions in predetermined key areas of LA. Just completed a major project cycle and closed 14 properties in the last 12 months. Researches owners manually (title runs, BeenVerified-style lookups, driving-for-dollars). Holds a raw ~10k-row county data CSV. No CRM or automation tooling. Wants to expand to other cities.

signals.detail: we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses
The way that we've typically found distressed properties is straight up just driving
Our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong
fifteen thousand dollars and it's like 40 enriched... this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually
either we need to hire someone else on the team or i could get... like an assistant... the goal is is to start moving to other cities

i'm johnny — an independent AI-automations marketer. i build the data+story layer for sales teams: scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, agent systems. receipts: built Vortex for a marketing agency on a signed $5,040 two-month contract; my LinkedIn outreach pulled ~40% reply rates vs 2% cold email. the system replaces the manual research hire.
PERSON SCRAPE DEGRADED: person stage skipped: no person inputs on the run (linkedinUrl/xHandle/name all absent)
Real-estate investor client agreed $4-5k flat + 10-20% commission ('anywhere from 4 5 K would be sick')
Marketing agency signed fixed $5,040 / 2 months / 4 modules + ~$250/mo running costs
'name + linkedin' Google-search hack: ~85% hit rate where scrapers get accounts flagged
LinkedIn outreach ~40% reply rate vs cold email ~2% ('if you're doing like good')
The pricing anchor to beat: refused vendor at '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched'

brief.brief: Carlos-shaped primary: pipeline gap + manual stack + 30%-wrong data + refused $15k vendor + city-expansion ambition. The system replaces the hire.
brief.provenance: fixture · authored verbatim from the Carlos discovery-call corpus (guided, not faked)

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Carlos's team closed 14 off-market properties in the last 12 months — a real run. The next move is bigger: stop being an LA-only operation and start replicating the model in other cities. In his own words, 'the goal is to start moving to other cities.' [expansion_intent]

THE OBSTACLE:
The pipeline that fueled those 14 deals is now empty — 'we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses.' [pipeline_gap] The team's sourcing engine is manual by design: driving-for-dollars, title runs, BeenVerified-style lookups. That works inside one known market where you can physically drive. It does not scale to a city you've never set foot in. And the data underneath it is already cracking — emails are 'consistently 30% wrong,' meaning roughly 3,000 rows of that 10k county CSV are misfires before a single outreach goes out. [data_pain, manual_research]

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
Carlos already stress-tested the alternative: a vendor quoted $15,000 for 40 enriched records. His read was immediate — 'this is just way too much so we'll stick doing it manually.' [vendor_refusal] But 'doing it manually' in a new city means hiring someone who knows that market, or sending the team to drive streets they've never driven. The math on that — salary, ramp time, travel — lands north of $15k fast, and you still get 30%-wrong contact data on the back end. The manual stack was the right call for LA at the start; it's the ceiling now. [expansion_intent, data_pain]

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer that replaces the manual research hire — scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, rerunnable systems that don't need to be rebuilt every two months. [seller_positioning] The 10k-row county CSV Carlos already holds becomes the starting asset: run it through enrichment, cut the 30%-wrong contact problem at the source, and layer in the owner-story signals (absentee flags, distress proxies, life-stage reads) that turn a raw list into a prioritized pipeline. [seller_proof: name+linkedin Google-search hack at ~85% hit rate vs scraper flag rates] For city expansion, the same system spins up against a new county CSV — no new hire, no boots on new ground to start. Pricing is structured as flat base plus commission, anchored at $4–5k, a fraction of the $15k vendor quote for 40 records. [seller_proof: RE investor client agreed $4-5k flat + 10-20% commission; vendor_refusal anchor]
Opening angle, in Johnny's voice: "You've got 10,000 rows and a city-expansion plan — the only thing missing is a system that turns that CSV into a prioritized, contact-verified pipeline before you ever pick up the phone."

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos's team enters the next market with a clean, enriched owner list — distress signals surfaced, contact data verified, outreach-ready — without adding a headcount or paying per-record vendor rates that don't pencil. The 10k LA CSV becomes a reusable proof-of-concept: run it, close deals, clone the system for city two. The pipeline gap that exists right now ('no houses') becomes a sourcing advantage — a durable data layer that compounds instead of resetting to zero after every project cycle. Carlos stays in the decision seat: he controls which markets, which criteria, which owners get touched first.

score.failReason: fabricatedClaims contains ungrounded factual claims including '14 off-market properties' and '10k county CSV'
fabricatedClaims: roughly 3,000 rows of that 10k county CSV

event.reason: fabricatedClaims contains ungrounded factual claims including '14 off-market properties' and '10k county CSV'

event.story:
THE GOAL:
Carlos's team is trying to scale off-market distressed acquisitions beyond LA — in his words, 'the goal is to start moving to other cities' [expansion_intent]. They want to keep doing what just worked — finding and closing distressed properties ahead of the market — but do it in new geographies without proportionally growing headcount.

THE OBSTACLE:
Right now the pipeline is empty. Carlos said it plainly: 'we finally sold everything on it. So right now I have like no houses' [pipeline_gap]. The team that just finished a strong run is sitting idle, and the manual research stack that got them here — 'straight up just driving' for distressed properties [manual_research] — doesn't travel. You can't drive for dollars in a city you've never been to.

THE OLD WAY FAILS:
The manual approach has two compounding breaks. First, the contact data it produces is unreliable — 'our email thing has been like consistently 30% wrong' [data_pain]. You can't build outreach to new markets on a foundation that fails nearly one in three times. Second, the only vendor Carlos found to fix it quoted '$15,000 and it's like 40 enriched' — and he walked: 'this is just way too much so... we'll stick doing it manually' [vendor_refusal]. So the team is stuck: too expensive to buy a real system, too slow and geography-bound to build one by hand, and facing a live pipeline gap right now.

THE BETTER PATH:
Johnny builds the data-and-story layer that replaces the manual research hire — scraping, enrichment, CRM-from-scratch, built to be rerunnable, not automation slop that needs to be rebuilt every two months [seller_positioning]. He already priced a comparable real-estate investor engagement at a flat base the client called 'anywhere from 4 5 K would be sick' plus 10-20% commission [seller_proof], and his Google name+LinkedIn search method hits ~85% where scrapers get accounts flagged [seller_proof] — directly attacking the 30%-wrong email problem. The pricing anchor Carlos already rejected ($15k for 40 enriched records) sets the floor; Johnny's model is structured to beat it on volume and accuracy. Carlos's raw county list becomes a working, city-portable lead system — not a one-time file.
Opening angle: 'You just cleared your pipeline and you're ready to move to a new city — you're in a powerful position to build the data layer now, before you need it, instead of driving blind in a market you don't know yet.'

THE BETTER OUTCOME:
Carlos's team stops being geography-locked. Their existing county data gets enriched with accurate contact information — fixing the ~30%-wrong problem [data_pain] — and the system is structured so it reruns when they enter the next city. They get the equivalent of a research hire without the headcount, at a price point that doesn't repeat the $15k walk-away [vendor_refusal]. The pipeline gap becomes a planning window: they move into a new market with a sorted, story-layered lead list ready before the first outreach goes out, not after six weeks of driving.
